questions.question: Genghis Khan founded which empire — the largest contiguous land empire in history?
questions.explanation: Genghis Khan founded the Mongol Empire in 1206 and expanded it across Central Asia, China, Persia, and into Eastern Europe. At its peak under his successors, the Mongol Empire covered 24 million square kilometers — the largest contiguous land empire in history. Genghis Khan is estimated to be a direct ancestor of approximately 0.5% of the world's male population today.
questions.memory_hook: Mongol = Massive. The Mongol Empire was so large that today roughly 1 in 200 men worldwide carries Genghis Khan's DNA. He didn't just conquer territory — he literally spread himself across it.

questions.question: The Wright Brothers made the first successful powered airplane flight in 1903 at which location?
questions.explanation: Orville Wright made the first successful powered, sustained, and controlled airplane flight on December 17, 1903, at Kitty Hawk, North Carolina. The flight lasted 12 seconds and covered 120 feet. They chose Kitty Hawk for its steady winds and soft sandy landing areas. Four flights were made that day, the longest lasting 59 seconds.
questions.memory_hook: Kitty Hawk, Kitty = cat, cat = curious, curiosity took flight. Also: KH = Kitty Hawk, and the first flight was tiny — 120 feet, less than the wingspan of a modern 747. Humanity went from 120 feet to the moon in 66 years.

questions.question: Johannes Gutenberg's invention of the movable-type printing press around 1440 occurred in which modern-day country?
questions.explanation: Johannes Gutenberg developed his movable-type printing press in Mainz, Germany, around 1440–1450. The Gutenberg Bible, printed around 1455, was the first major book printed in the West using this technology. The printing press is widely considered one of the most transformative inventions in human history, democratizing knowledge and enabling the Reformation and Scientific Revolution.
questions.memory_hook: Gutenberg = German. Both start with G. His press in Germany did what the internet did 550 years later — made information available to everyone. The Renaissance, Reformation, and Scientific Revolution all followed within 200 years.

questions.question: The Great Fire of London occurred in which year?
questions.explanation: The Great Fire of London began on September 2, 1666, in a bakery on Pudding Lane and burned for three days, destroying 13,200 houses, 87 churches, and most of the medieval City of London. Remarkably, only 6 deaths were officially recorded. The fire led to the rebuilding of London in brick and stone, and Christopher Wren redesigned St. Paul's Cathedral in its aftermath.
questions.memory_hook: 1666 = the number of the Beast (666) + 1000. Londoners at the time thought it was literally the apocalypse. The Great Fire of London in 1666 started on Pudding Lane — the most unfortunately named street in disaster history.

questions.question: Pelé is the only soccer player to have won how many FIFA World Cups?
questions.explanation: Pelé is the only player in history to win three FIFA World Cup championships (1958, 1962, and 1970) with Brazil. He won his first at just 17 years old in 1958, making him the youngest World Cup winner ever. He scored 1,279 goals in 1,363 games over his career and is widely considered the greatest footballer of all time.
questions.memory_hook: Pelé = 3 Cups. No one else has 3. He won his first at 17 — a teenager winning the World Cup. His last in 1970 is often called the greatest World Cup team performance ever.

questions.question: The Kentucky Derby horse race is nicknamed 'The Run for the' what?
questions.explanation: The Kentucky Derby is nicknamed 'The Run for the Roses' because the winner is draped in a blanket of red roses — a tradition dating back to 1896. Held annually on the first Saturday in May at Churchill Downs in Louisville, Kentucky, the Derby is the first leg of horse racing's Triple Crown, followed by the Preakness Stakes and the Belmont Stakes.
questions.memory_hook: Derby = Roses. Red roses draped over a sweaty racehorse. The winner gets flowers — 554 of them sewn into a blanket. It's the most elegant tradition in a sport where the athletes are animals.

questions.question: Babe Ruth hit 714 home runs in his career. For how many years did this record stand?
questions.explanation: Babe Ruth hit his 714th and final career home run in 1935. The record stood until April 8, 1974, when Hank Aaron hit his 715th home run — a span of 39 years. Aaron went on to finish his career with 755 home runs. Barry Bonds later broke Aaron's record with 762, though his legacy is complicated by performance-enhancing drug allegations.
questions.memory_hook: Ruth's record lasted 39 years — set in the 1930s, broken in the 1970s. Three generations of baseball fans grew up thinking 714 was unbreakable. Hank Aaron got death threats for approaching it. The home run record has always carried enormous weight.

questions.question: Whitney Houston's iconic 'I Will Always Love You' was originally written and recorded by which other artist?
questions.explanation: Dolly Parton wrote and first recorded 'I Will Always Love You' in 1973 as a gentle country song. Whitney Houston's 1992 version for The Bodyguard soundtrack transformed it into a pop powerhouse. Parton reportedly cried when she heard Houston's rendition. The song earned Parton an estimated $10 million in royalties from Houston's version alone.
questions.memory_hook: Dolly wrote it, Whitney owned it. The quiet country goodbye became a stadium-shaking power ballad. Two completely different songs with the same words — that's the power of interpretation.

questions.question: The Silence of the Lambs (1991) is one of only three films to win all five major Academy Awards. Which actor played Hannibal Lecter?
questions.explanation: Anthony Hopkins won the Academy Award for Best Actor for playing Dr. Hannibal Lecter in The Silence of the Lambs — despite appearing on screen for only 16 minutes. The film swept the Big Five Oscars: Best Picture, Director, Actor, Actress, and Screenplay. Hopkins reportedly based his voice partly on HAL 9000 from 2001: A Space Odyssey.
questions.memory_hook: Hopkins won Best Actor for 16 minutes of screen time. That's roughly one minute of film per year he'd been acting. The most efficient Oscar performance in history — every second counted.

questions.question: Bob Dylan became the first musician to win the Nobel Prize in which category in 2016?
questions.explanation: Bob Dylan was awarded the Nobel Prize in Literature in 2016 — the first musician to receive the honor — 'for having created new poetic expressions within the great American song tradition.' Dylan initially didn't acknowledge the award and did not attend the ceremony, collecting his gold medal and diploma months later in a private meeting.
questions.memory_hook: Dylan won the Literature Nobel — not music, Literature. The Nobel Committee treated his lyrics as poetry. He responded by ignoring them for weeks. Bob Dylan has always done things on his own terms.
